notes.anchor: asking you to share it with Camillus
notes.note: C. Furius Camillus, a jurist and a mutual friend, whom Cicero used as a confidential adviser on the legal tangles of Tullia's marriage and dowry and of Terentia's will.

notes.anchor: A man called Agusius had come from Rhodes
notes.note: T. Agusius, a friend who had shared Cicero's exile, here serves as a courier bringing news from the East via Rhodes; Cicero forwards his letter by a faster traveller and refers Atticus to Agusius for oral details.

notes.anchor: my son Quintus had set out for Caesar
notes.note: Young Quintus, the nephew, had gone to Caesar in the East — the journey on which he denounced his uncle. "My son Quintus" (Quintum filium) marks him as the son, distinct from Cicero's brother Quintus named just below.

Dolabella, tribune in 47 BC, revived the radical politics of P. Clodius Pulcher — pushing debt-cancellation and erecting a statue of the dead demagogue who had once driven Cicero into exile. That his own son-in-law should take up Clodius's cause cut Cicero deeply.

notes.anchor: the new account-books, or the nocturnal raids, or Metella
notes.note: Grounds on which Tullia could have divorced Dolabella: his agitation for new account-books (cancellation of debts), the night-time street violence of his tribunate, and an affair with a noblewoman named Metella. This Metella's precise identity is uncertain and she is not the Caecilia Metella of the Pro Roscio Amerino.

notes.anchor: the third installment of the dowry
notes.note: A Roman dowry was commonly paid in three annual installments; on divorce the husband normally returned what he had received. Cicero fears Dolabella will demand the still-unpaid third installment of Tullia's dowry rather than refund the rest.
